does: This paper surveys vulnerabilities and defenses in large language models, systematically categorizing jailbreaking attacks into prompt-based, model-based, multimodal, and multilingual approaches, covering techniques like adversarial prompting, backdoor injection, and cross-modality exploits. It reviews corresponding defense mechanisms spanning prompt filtering, transformation, alignment techniques, multi-agent defenses, and self-regulation strategies, evaluating their effectiveness and limitations. The work also examines metrics and benchmarks for assessing LLM safety and robustness, highlighting measurement challenges in interactive settings and dataset biases that complicate evaluation.
connects: This work builds on emerging concerns about LLM safety that surfaced as these models gained widespread deployment. It extends prior research on adversarial examples and prompt injection attacks in neural systems to the specific context of large language models, integrating findings from work on model alignment, backdoor attacks in deep learning, and adversarial robustness literature. The paper synthesizes insights from multiple threat vectors—combining research on prompt injection vulnerabilities, multimodal attack surfaces from vision-language models, and multilingual exploitation—that had previously been studied in isolation, while building on established defense paradigms like input filtering and model training techniques.
enables: This comprehensive review establishes a foundation for more systematic vulnerability assessment and defense development in LLMs. By synthesizing attack and defense taxonomies, it enables researchers to identify gaps in current protections and design more robust evaluation benchmarks. The discussion of measurement challenges points toward developing better metrics for quantifying attack success in interactive contexts and creating less biased safety datasets. The work sets up opportunities for developing integrated defense frameworks that handle multiple attack vectors simultaneously, advancing toward certified robustness in language models and informing deployment policies for safety-critical applications.
